Notice Of Motion And Motion To Stay This Case Pending Silicosis Cases Petition For Coordination; Declaration Of Andrea A. Lowe
Off Calendar Jun-27-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances via Zoom Conference:
Juanita Tirado, Esq., of Brayton Purcell LLP. Tel:415.898.1555 for plaintiff.
Ronald S. Whitaker, Esq., of lydecker LLP. Tel:213.226.7068 for defendant Caesarstone,USA.

Hearing is held. Defendant counsel contests the tentative ruling regarding deferring to the coordination Judge as to the issuance of a stay in this matter.The matter argued/discussed. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Caesarstone USA, Inc.'s motion to stay this case pending adjudication of the coordination petition is denied.

The statutes governing coordination vest the coordination judge with the power to stay the proceedings. CCP 404.5 provides that "Pending any determination of whether coordination is appropriate, the judge making that determination may stay any action being considered for, or affecting an action being considered for, coordination." (Code Civ. Procedure section 404.5.) Though section 404.5 does not deprive the court of the authority to stay proceedings, it evinces the Legislature's intent for the coordination judge to decide whether cases are stayed pre-coordination.

Order is Signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-08-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. DAN MORSE, Esq. of Brayton Purcell LLP (tel: 415-898-1555) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. ERIN PAULEY, Esq. of Barnes & Thornburg LLP (tel: 310-284-3880) for Defendant Costco Wholesale Corporation.
Atty. JAMES SPRATT, Esq. of Lydecker (tel: 213-226-7068) for Defendant Caesarstone USA, Inc.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT COSTCO WHOLESALE CORPORATION's DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

Matter not argued/contested. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Costco Wholesale Corporation's demurrer to plaintiff Rafael Garcia-Guerra's first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled.

Garcia contracted lung disease after working as a cutter, fabricator, and installer of stone products from 1997 to 2022 and allegedly inhaling the silica from stone dust. Garcia sued several dozen defendants including Costco, which allegedly sold stone products containing silica. Costco now demurs under CCP 430.10(e) to the fourth cause of action for fraudulent concealment.

The elements of fraudulent concealment are: (1) defendant concealed or suppressed a material fact, (2) defendant was under a duty to disclose the fact to plaintiff, (3) defendant intentionally concealed or suppressed the fact with the intent to defraud plaintiff, (4) plaintiff was unaware of the fact and would not have acted if he had known, and (5) resulting damage. [Citation.] (Jones v. ConocoPhillips Co. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1187, 1198.) Concealment constitutes actionable fraud when, e.g., the defendant had exclusive knowledge of material facts not known to the plaintiff. (Id. at 1199.) Though fraud must be pled with specificity, less is required to plead fraud by concealment. (Id.)

Costco argues Garcia fails to meet the heightened pleading standard for fraud. Garcia's allegations, however, contain similar levels of specificity as those upheld in Jones, supra, 198 Cal.App.4th at 1200 [complaint alleged manufacturer was aware of its product's toxicity, and owed a duty to disclose based on its knowledge of material facts unavailable to the plaintiff]. Here too Garcia alleges Costco sold stone products containing silica, and that C-level executives were aware these products contained silica. (FAC 71, 556-558.) Costco contends Jones and its ilk are distinguishable because they concerned manufacturers who were presumably aware of the hazards posed by their products, whereas retailers are not. Costco does not cite authority distinguishing Jones on this basis. Thus, the principles in Jones seemingly apply. Costco, a large and sophisticated retailer, could have known and allegedly did know material facts regarding the toxic properties of the products it chose to sell that were not available to Garcia. Discovery may reveal the extent of this knowledge. Further, the complaint gives adequate notice to Costco that it allegedly concealed the toxic properties of the products it sold. (See Jones, supra, 198 Cal.App.4th at 1200 [citing Quelimane Co. v. Stewart Title Guaranty Co. (1998) 19 Cal.4th 26, 38.)

Next, Costco contends that Garcia merely speculates and fails to allege he was even exposed to a countertop sold by Costco. (Mem. 10:21-24.) Garcia, however, adequately alleges he "cut, drilled, polished? etc." defendants' silica-containing stone products, which includes Costco. (See FAC 71; Bockrath v. Aldrich Chemical Co., Inc. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 71, 83 ["We presume that plaintiff here was aware of his duty to pursue his suit in good faith, and in the absence of contrary evidence, that he did so"].) The demurrer is overruled.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling with a cover letter stating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302)

MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS
Off Calendar May-14-2024 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. SCOTT BRUST, Esq. of Metzger Law Group, APLC (tel: 562-437-4499) for Plaintiffs.
Atty. JOSEPH TERESI, Esq. of Latham & Watkins LLP (tel: 213-485-1234) for Defendant Stylenquaza LLC.
Atty. VARUN SABHARWAL, Esq. of Gordon Rees Scully Mansukhani LLP (tel: 619-696-6700) for Defendant MS International, Inc.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT STYLENQUAZA, LLC's MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

Matter discussed. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant STYLENQUAZA LLC's motion to quash is granted. Plaintiff fails to meet its burden of proof to demonstrate specific jurisdiction. (Edmunds v. Superior Court (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 221, 228.)

To establish specific jurisdiction, "[t]he plaintiff's claims must arise out of or relate to the defendant's contacts' with the forum." Ford Motor Co. v. Mont. Eighth Judicial Dist. Court, 592 U.S. 351, 359 (2021). In addition, a manufacturer can be haled into court where it makes efforts to serve the California market and the sale is not an isolated occurrence. (Id. at 363.) In Cassiar Mining Corp. v. Superior Court (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 550, for example, the court found specific jurisdiction where the defendant sold thousands of tons of raw asbestos to California operations for 38 years.

Here, plaintiffs fail to show that their claims arose from defendant's de minimis contacts with California. Defendant's isolated and sporadic California contacts do not qualify as knowing availment of California benefits to warrant the exercise of specific jurisdiction. (See Nguyen and Morse Decls.)

Plaintiff's request for jurisdictional discovery is denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
